rules.name: custom_metric_authoring
rules.overview:
The 28-metric catalog covers the common cases, but partners with domain-specific quality needs (e.g. "does this response cite the correct contract clause?") will want to author their own metrics. The SDK ships two LLM-as-judge authoring styles and helpers for both.
Read this when the partner says: "I need a guardrail for X and it's not in the catalog."

use_existing_if.case: A safety / RAG / output-quality concern is already covered by one of the 28 catalog metrics. Tuning the threshold is usually enough.
The judgment is binary "safe vs unsafe" — Granite Guardian's safety metrics handle this better than a custom judge.
The criterion can be expressed as a regex or keyword list — use KeywordDetection / RegexDetection instead.

author_custom_if.case: The judgment is domain-specific: "does this answer correctly cite our internal policy?" "does this response use the partner's brand voice?"
The criterion is a multi-level rubric: High/Medium/Low or complete/partial/incomplete.
The partner has tried tuning catalog metrics and they're still missing the target behavior.

style.name: prompt_template
style.suitability: full control over the LLM prompt
style.when: You need to inject custom context, multi-turn examples, or carefully crafted instructions. You're comfortable maintaining a prompt template.
style.signature:
from ibm_watsonx_gov.metrics import LLMAsJudgeMetric
my_metric = LLMAsJudgeMetric( name="contract_clause_correctness", llm_judge=judge, prompt_template=""" You are evaluating whether an AI response correctly cites the relevant contract clause.
Question: {input_text} AI Response: {generated_text} Reference Contract: {context}
Return: correct / partially_correct / incorrect """, input_fields=["input_text", "generated_text", "context"], options={"correct": 1, "partially_correct": 0.5, "incorrect": 0}, )

style.name: criteria_description_plus_Option
style.suitability: simple rubric, SDK auto-generates the prompt
style.when: The judgment is a short Y/N or 3-tier rubric. You don't want to maintain a full prompt.
style.signature:
from ibm_watsonx_gov.entities.criteria import Option from real_time_guardrails.core.custom_metrics import build_criteria_judge
my_metric = build_criteria_judge( name="brand_voice", display_name="Brand Voice", criteria_description="Does the {generated_text} match our brand voice (professional, concise, warm)?", options=[ Option(name="Yes", description="On-brand: professional, concise, warm.", value=1.0), Option(name="No", description="Off-brand.", value=0.0), ], judge=judge, )
style.helper: build_criteria_judge() in core/custom_metrics.py — wraps LLMAsJudgeMetric for the criteria style.

two_authoring_styles.side_by_side_table: | Aspect | prompt_template | criteria + Option | |---|---|---| | Prompt authoring | You write it | SDK generates it | | Best for | Complex judgments needing examples or context shaping | Short rubrics: Yes/No, High/Medium/Low, etc. | | Lines of code | ~15-25 | ~8-12 | | Latency | Same (1 LLM call) | Same (1 LLM call) | | Maintenance burden | Higher (the prompt is a long string) | Lower (just Options + 1-line criteria) | | Recommended starting point | If criteria-style doesn't fit | Default — try this first |

registration_in_registry.description: For a custom metric to be callable via `ev.evaluate(metrics=["My Custom Metric"])`, it must be in the MetricRegistry. The registry is built once at evaluator init time from `build_registry(config)` in core/metrics.py.

option_a.name: post_init_registration
option_a.description: Simplest: append the entry to `ev.registry` after construction. No need to fork the package.
option_a.code:
from real_time_guardrails import GuardrailsEvaluator from real_time_guardrails.core.registry import MetricEntry from real_time_guardrails.core.thresholds import ThresholdSpec, Direction
ev = GuardrailsEvaluator()
my_metric_entry = MetricEntry( name="Brand Voice", metric=my_metric, # built via build_criteria_judge() above category="quality", column_name="brand_voice.llm_as_judge", threshold_spec=ThresholdSpec(value=0.5, direction=Direction.LOW_IS_RISK, flag_value=0.7), required_fields=frozenset({"generated_text"}), description="Custom rubric: on-brand vs off-brand response check.", ) ev.registry._by_name[my_metric_entry.name] = my_metric_entry # The registry exposes its internal dict; mutate carefully — re-attach known_metrics: ev._threshold_resolver.attach_known_metrics(set(ev.registry.names))
bundle = ev.evaluate(generated_text="...", metrics=["Brand Voice"])

option_b.name: patch_build_registry
option_b.description: For partners who fork the SDK: add the entry directly to build_registry() in core/metrics.py alongside the 28 catalog metrics.

caveat.name: judge_model_selection
caveat.description: Default judge is `llama-3-3-70b-instruct` on watsonx.ai. Override via env var `WXG_JUDGE_MODEL_ID` or by building the LLMJudge directly with a different `WxAIFoundationModel(model_id=...)`.
caveat.recommendation: For simple Yes/No rubrics, smaller models (granite-3-8b-instruct) work and cost less. For nuanced multi-tier judgments, stick with 70B+.

caveat.name: latency_cost
caveat.description: Every LLM-judge invocation is a synchronous watsonx.ai call: 1-3 seconds + token cost. For high-volume traffic, sample (e.g. evaluate every 10th request) instead of evaluating every request.

caveat.name: token_cost
caveat.description: Llama-3.3-70B: ~200-500 input + ~10 output tokens per call. At watsonx.ai's per-token pricing, sustained traffic can add up quickly. Monitor via `WXG_PROJECT_ID`'s usage dashboard.

caveat.name: determinism
caveat.description: LLM judges are not perfectly deterministic. The same input may produce slightly different scores across calls. For high-stakes decisions, use the binary Yes/No rubric (less score variance than continuous scoring) and set thresholds conservatively.

caveat.name: prompt_injection_in_inputs
caveat.description: The LLM judge sees `input_text` + `generated_text` etc. as part of its prompt. Adversarial inputs can attempt to manipulate the judge. Mitigation: run safety guardrails (Jailbreak Detection) BEFORE the custom judge fires, so jailbreak-style inputs are blocked before they reach the judge.

reference_example.see: .bob/reference-payloads/custom_metric_example.py — working example with both styles + post-init registration.

replacing_built_in_llm_judges.overview:
The SDK ships 3 LLM-as-judge metrics with OPINIONATED PROMPTS: - Answer Completeness (LLM Judge) — our prompt in core/custom_metrics.py - Conciseness (LLM Judge) — our prompt in core/custom_metrics.py - Tool Call Relevance — gov SDK's built-in prompt
Partners may want different rubrics — different brand voice, stricter compliance criteria, multi-language judges, etc. The same authoring pattern works for REPLACEMENT as for new metrics: build a new LLMAsJudgeMetric (either style), and register it under the SAME NAME so it overrides the catalog entry.
replacing_built_in_llm_judges.pattern:
from real_time_guardrails import GuardrailsEvaluator from real_time_guardrails.core.custom_metrics import build_criteria_judge from real_time_guardrails.core.registry import MetricEntry from real_time_guardrails.core.thresholds import ThresholdSpec, Direction from ibm_watsonx_gov.entities.criteria import Option from ibm_watsonx_gov.entities.foundation_model import WxAIFoundationModel from ibm_watsonx_gov.entities.llm_judge import LLMJudge
ev = GuardrailsEvaluator()
# 1. Build the partner's replacement metric — same name as the built-in. judge = LLMJudge(model=WxAIFoundationModel( model_id="llama-3-3-70b-instruct", project_id=ev._config.project_id, )) partner_completeness = build_criteria_judge( name="answer_completeness", display_name="Answer Completeness (LLM Judge)", # SAME display name criteria_description=( "Does the {generated_text} cite a specific section " "of our compliance policy (e.g., 'per Section 4.2')?" ), options=[ Option(name="Yes", description="Cites a section.", value=1.0), Option(name="No", description="Generic answer without citation.", value=0.0), ], judge=judge, )
# 2. Override the catalog entry by the same display name. ev.registry._by_name["Answer Completeness (LLM Judge)"] = MetricEntry( name="Answer Completeness (LLM Judge)", metric=partner_completeness, category="quality", column_name="answer_completeness.llm_as_judge", # same column threshold_spec=ThresholdSpec(value=0.5, direction=Direction.LOW_IS_RISK), required_fields=frozenset({"input_text", "generated_text"}), description="(partner override) Compliance citation check.", )
# 3. Now ev.evaluate(metrics=["Answer Completeness (LLM Judge)"]) uses # the partner's prompt instead of ours.

skip_built_in_judges_entirely.how: Simplest: don't set WXG_PROJECT_ID. The registry omits all 3 LLM-judge metrics. Partner gets 25 of 28 metrics, no LLM-judge in the catalog at all.
skip_built_in_judges_entirely.when: Partner doesn't want token cost or non-determinism. Common for high-volume / low-margin deployments.

when_to_advise_against_authoring.case: Partner wants to add a custom safety check that overlaps with existing safety metrics (HAP / Harm / SocialBias) — recommend tuning thresholds on the existing metrics instead.
Partner wants determinism / 100% reproducibility — LLM judges can't deliver this. Recommend rule-based or regex-based custom checks via the Pattern category.
Partner needs to enforce schema or structured output — that's a JSON validation problem, not a guardrails problem. Recommend pydantic / json-schema validation before calling our SDK.
